content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

January 15, 1997

Stephen Gilbert CRNA, MS
2062 Sierra Mesa Drive
Camarillo, CA  93010

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-96-352

Dear Mr. Gilbert:

     This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
campaign provisions of Proposition 208 and the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

     Does a campaign committee that is not candidate controlled
have to spend all of its money before January 1, 1997, or can that
money be held in account and dispersed after that date in
compliance with Proposition 208?

CONCLUSION

     There is no requirement in Proposition 208 which would
mandate the committee to spend its funds prior to January 1, 1997.

DISCUSSION

     Proposition 208 is effective as of January 1, 1997.  Some of
the major provisions of Proposition 208 are summarized below.
Proposition 208 does not require a campaign committee to spend its
funds before its effective date.  Therefore, you may spend the
funds out of your existing account consistent with the provisions
of Proposition 208 and Sections 89510-89518, which were not
repealed by Proposition 208.


     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All references are to
the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  All references to
regulations are to Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-96-352
Page 2


     On or after January 1, 1997, the contribution limits that
apply to committees will apply to contributions your committee
receives. If your committee makes contributions to candidates,
the committee may not accept contributions from any person
totaling more than $500 per year. (Section 85301(d).) If your
organization has more than 100 members, receives no more than
$50.00 in contributions from any person, and has in existence
for more than six months, the committee may qualify as a small
contributor committee and thus be permitted to give higher
contributions to candidates. (Sections 85203 and 85302.)
Finally, if your committee makes independent expenditures of more
than $1,000 in support or opposition to any candidate, the
committee may not accept a contribution in excess of $250.
(Section 85301; Ford Advice Letter, No. A-96-351.)

     I have enclosed a chart summarizing Proposition 208, as well
as a copy of the initiative itself for your convenience. If you
have any other questions regarding Proposition 208, please contact
me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Lynda Doherty
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Legal Division

SGC:ld

Enclosures

have to spend all of its money before January 1,
1997,
or can that
money be held in account and dispersed after that date in
compliance with Proposition 208?

CONCLUSION: There is no requirement in Proposition 208 which would
mandate the committee to spend its funds prior to January 1,
1997.
